event: Austronesian-descended Lapita voyagers reach the western islands of the region, beginning the human settlement of the central Pacific.
Polynesian seafarers settle the Marquesas Islands, establishing one of the earliest population centers in eastern Polynesia.
Settlers from the Marquesas spread to the Society Islands, founding communities on Tahiti, Raiatea, and neighboring islands.
Raiatea's sacred site of Taputapuatea develops into a major religious and political center of eastern Polynesia.
Marquesan voyagers undertake long-distance migrations that help seed populations in Hawaii and other parts of the Polynesian triangle.
Chiefdoms across the Society Islands grow more hierarchical, with powerful ariʻi clans and elaborate marae temple complexes.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition sights Puka-Puka in the Tuamotus, the first recorded European encounter with the islands.
Spanish navigator Alvaro de Mendana de Neira reaches the Marquesas and names them for the Marquis of Canete.
Pedro Fernandes de Queiros sails through the Tuamotu Archipelago during his Pacific voyage.
British captain Samuel Wallis arrives at Tahiti aboard HMS Dolphin, the first European to land on the island.
French explorer Louis-Antoine de Bougainville visits Tahiti and claims it for France, popularizing the island in Europe.
Captain James Cook anchors at Tahiti to observe the transit of Venus, aided by the navigator-priest Tupaia.
HMS Bounty arrives at Tahiti to collect breadfruit plants, a stay that preceded the famous mutiny the following year.
London Missionary Society Protestant missionaries land at Tahiti, beginning the Christianization of the islands.
Pomare II defeats rival chiefs at the Battle of Feʻi Pi and unifies Tahiti under his rule, adopting Christianity.
The Pomare Code, a set of Christian-influenced laws, is promulgated for the kingdom of Tahiti.
Pomare IV (Aimata) becomes queen of Tahiti, reigning for half a century through the colonial transition.
France declares a protectorate over Tahiti and annexes the Marquesas Islands, sparking conflict with Britain.
The Franco-Tahitian War erupts as Tahitians resist French control, with fighting continuing for several years.
Queen Pomare IV returns to Tahiti and accepts the French protectorate, ending armed resistance.
King Pomare V cedes Tahiti and its dependencies to France, which annexes them as a colony.
France formally annexes the Leeward (Society) Islands after suppressing local resistance, completing colonial control.
The scattered French possessions are administratively unified as the Etablissements francais de l'Oceanie (French Establishments in Oceania).
German cruisers bombard Papeete during World War I, destroying part of the town.
United States forces establish a base on Bora Bora during World War II to support Pacific supply lines.
The territory becomes an overseas territory of France, and its inhabitants gain French citizenship.
The territory is officially renamed French Polynesia.
French Polynesia votes to remain within the French Community in a referendum.
France selects the Tuamotu atolls of Moruroa and Fangataufa as sites for its nuclear weapons testing program.
The Centre d'Experimentation du Pacifique is established, transforming the territory's economy and society.
France conducts its first atmospheric nuclear test at Moruroa Atoll.
France ends atmospheric nuclear testing and shifts to underground tests at Moruroa and Fangataufa.
French Polynesia gains partial internal autonomy under a new statute.
An expanded autonomy statute creates a Territorial Assembly and the post of territorial president.
France's resumption of underground nuclear tests at Moruroa triggers violent riots in Papeete and global protests.
France permanently ends nuclear testing in the Pacific and signs the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty.
A French constitutional reform designates French Polynesia an overseas collectivity with broad self-government.
A new autonomy statute grants French Polynesia the status of an overseas country, but a period of political instability begins.
France enacts the Morin Law to compensate victims of its Pacific nuclear tests.
The United Nations General Assembly reinscribes French Polynesia on its list of non-self-governing territories.
UNESCO inscribes the Taputapuatea marae complex on Raiatea as a World Heritage Site.
French President Emmanuel Macron acknowledges France's nuclear-testing debt to French Polynesia during a state visit.
Pro-independence leader Moetai Brotherson becomes president of French Polynesia after his party wins territorial elections.
Teahupoʻo in Tahiti hosts the surfing events of the Paris Olympic Games, spotlighting the territory globally.
